exemplar.title: M25 #4: Do the ever-improving tools of an area of knowledge always result in improved knowledge? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #4: Do the ever-improving tools of an area of knowledge always result in improved knowledge? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a clear connection to the prescribed title throughout and consistently refers to two specified areas of knowledge (Human Sciences and the Arts).

Employs a range of concrete examplesâinternational relations theories, demographic-transition modelling, theatre lighting developments, AI-generated musicâthat demonstrate engagement with the titleâs key ideas.
Shows awareness of alternative perspectives (e.g. Realism vs. Constructivism in the Human Sciences; benefits vs. uncertainties of AI in the Arts).
Clearly identifies and compares distinct areas of knowledge, satisfying the requirement to link multiple domains to the title.
Ends with a concise summary that directly restates the core claim that improved tools do not necessarily guarantee improved knowledge, addressing the prescribed title explicitly.
weaknesses: Examples are largely descriptive and function as background rather than as analysed evidence; the essay stops short of probing the epistemic foundations of the cases presented.
Arguments remain limited in depth, with assertions (e.g. ânew theories both broaden and limit knowledge,â âAI challenges authenticityâ) insufficiently developed or evaluated.
Lacks explicit TOK terminology and a clearly framed knowledge question to sharpen the theoretical underpinning of the discussion.

Conclusion restates examples without synthesizing broader implications or establishing criteria for judging when tools genuinely improve knowledge; no clear counterclaims or conditions under which tools might reliably enhance understanding.
Little evaluation of underlying assumptions (e.g. demographic-model metrics, ontological commitments in IR theories, authenticity criteria in art) and their impact on the validity of knowledge.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay satisfies the descriptor for band 3â4: it is connected to the title and links to areas of knowledge, offers recognisable arguments supported by examples, and shows some awareness of alternative viewpoints. However, its treatment remains largely descriptive, with limited depth of analysis and superficial evaluation of epistemic issues. These features justify awarding a score of 4, reflecting a satisfactory but underdeveloped engagement with the prescribed title.

annotations.comment: While the historical provenance of theories is noted, there is little evaluation of their epistemic limitationsâsuch as how reliance on state-centric data in Realism may skew understanding of global politics.
The essay acknowledges contrasting perspectives (Realism vs. Constructivism), demonstrating awareness of alternative viewpoints within the Human Sciences.

The argument about Postcolonialism challenging earlier theories is promising but lacks a clear link to the degree tools (theories) improve knowledge; include analysis of how colonial bias may distort or enrich knowledge.
The transition to the first example (âglobal politics theoriesâ) outlines tool use but stops short of linking it back to how these theories guarantee (or fail to guarantee) improved knowledge.

This introduction offers a broad definition of tools but lacks reference to TOK terminology (e.g., Ways of Knowing, knowledge frameworks). Explicitly linking tools to key TOK concepts will strengthen the theoretical foundation.

The description of Realism and Constructivism is informative but remains descriptive. To elevate the analysis, the student should examine how differing ontological assumptions shape what counts as valid political knowledge.

The title accurately restates the prescribed question but would benefit from an explicit knowledge question framed in TOK terms (e.g., âTo what extent do tools shape the validity of knowledge?â) to sharpen the focus of the essay.
The claim about mathematics treating theorems as objective improvements requires deeper analysis: how do axiomatic assumptions affect the reliability of mathematical knowledge?
The student clearly identifies two areas of knowledgeâHuman Sciences and the Artsâwhich aligns with the titleâs requirement to compare distinct domains.
The cyclical model of theory evolution is interesting but requires examples of how new frameworks both advance and impede knowledge acquisition in specific contexts to deepen the argument.
The conclusion restates examples but falls short of synthesizing their broader implications for the nature of knowledge or proposing criteria by which tools can be judged as improving understanding.

The demographic transition model is well chosen as a tool, but the student should unpack its underlying assumptions (e.g., economic development metrics) to assess how these shape the accuracy of population forecasts.
Noting the modelâs inability to account for globalization is valid; strengthen this by citing specific cases where unmodeled variables led to significant predictive errors.
The essay insightfully recognizes that the scope of a toolâin this case demographic modelingâdirectly influences the validity and applicability of the knowledge produced.
Historical context of lighting evolution is clear, yet the student should evaluate how new lighting technologies alter interpretive frameworks, audience perception, and thus the knowledge conveyed.
The theatre lighting example is descriptive of technical developments but needs an explicit link to how evolving lighting tools modify the Ways of Knowing and shared understanding in the Arts.
The AI-in-music case is highly relevant, but the discussion of authenticity would benefit from linking to TOK concepts (e.g., the role of intent and authorship in knowledge production).
Using the Bad Bunny/Bad Gyal AI example effectively illustrates how technological tools can introduce uncertainty about the provenance and authenticity of artistic knowledge.
The conclusion introduces high-level ideas but lacks discussion of the implications within each AOK; integrating a brief evaluation would add depth.
There is no clear thesis response at the end; the student should assert a position on whether ever-improving tools do or do not guarantee improved knowledge, supported by the examples discussed.
The conclusion maintains focus on the titleâs core idea, demonstrating consistency in addressing how tools and knowledge interact across both areas of knowledge.
The final summary clearly states that improved tools do not necessarily guarantee improved knowledge, directly addressing the prescribed title.
No counterclaim or mention of conditions under which tools might reliably improve knowledge; including this would demonstrate balanced evaluation.
The phrase âunderlying ideaâ is vague; the student should define key terms (e.g., âimprovementâ) to clarify the conclusionâs claim.
Consider adding a final suggestion for future inquiry, such as criteria for evaluating when a toolâs enhancements genuinely advance knowledge in different AOKs.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
